root_cause: A bad deploy. The `web` service (sreft-web) was running RELEASE=v2, which contains a regression: the process starts and stays up but never becomes healthy — it fails its readiness check (/healthz -> 503) and returns HTTP 500 on the /orders checkout path. Because the container was running (just unhealthy), a naive process/container check looked fine while user-facing traffic failed. The database and the unrelated `payments` neighbor (running the known-good RELEASE=v1) were healthy throughout, confirming the fault was isolated to the v2 code, not infrastructure or data. The v2 container logs stated this explicitly: 'RELEASE=v2 failing readiness and returning 500s on /orders (regression introduced in this release; previous good release was v1)'.
actions_taken: Investigated: `docker ps` showed sreft-web Up but its /healthz returned 503 and /orders returned 500 while / returned 404; `docker logs sreft-web` revealed the v2 readiness/500 regression and named v1 as the last good release. Confirmed the docker-compose project (tier0/docker-compose.yaml) pinned web to RELEASE=v2 and that the healthy payments neighbor ran RELEASE=v1. Remediated with the least-risky fix — a rollback: edited only the web service's RELEASE env from v2 to v1 in docker-compose.yaml and ran `docker compose up -d web` to recreate just that one service. Verified recovery: web now logs 'RELEASE=v1 listening on :8080', /orders returns 200 and /healthz returns 200. Left load, payments, and operator containers untouched and confirmed payments still healthy on v1. No destructive actions taken.
postmortem: Blameless summary: Checkout failed for users because release v2 of the web service shipped a regression that made it fail readiness and return 500s on /orders. The container stayed running, so basic liveness signals stayed green while real traffic broke — a classic 'up but not healthy' failure. Impact was limited to the web tier; the database and the payments service were unaffected. Resolution was a rollback to the last-known-good release v1, restoring healthy checks and 200 responses on checkout. This was a process/tooling gap, not an individual's fault. Follow-ups to prevent recurrence: (1) gate deploys on the readiness/health endpoint so an unhealthy release never receives production traffic (fail the rollout instead of leaving it 'out but broken'); (2) add health-based alerting on /healthz and on 5xx rate for /orders rather than relying on container-running status; (3) adopt progressive delivery (canary/blue-green with automatic rollback) so a bad release is caught before full exposure; (4) ensure quick, well-documented rollback of a single service as demonstrated here.
